summary: DeVault critiques GitHub's new notifications UI as a regression in usability compared to the old design. He walks through his daily workflow of triaging notifications and shows how the new interface breaks efficient scanning patterns: notifications are no longer grouped by repository, action buttons are hidden until hover, and real-time updates cause items to disappear unpredictably. He argues the redesign prioritizes visual polish over the muscle-memory-driven efficiency that power users depend on. He concludes pessimistically that negative feedback rarely reverses these rollouts because team pride prevents course correction.
key_points: The old UI enabled efficient top-to-bottom scanning with notifications grouped by repository and action buttons always visible
The new UI forces eyes to traverse wide whitespace, hides action buttons behind hover states, and removes repository grouping, breaking established workflows
Real-time updates cause notifications to disappear as you interact with them in other tabs, turning triage into a frustrating race condition
A 160-pixel sticky header wastes 14% of vertical space and is difficult to remove even with custom user styles
Feature rollouts at large companies rarely get reversed because team pride overrides user feedback
money_quotes: Scanning the list and middle-clicking every other message becomes a Sisyphean task.
Negative feedback almost never leads to cancelling the roll-out of a change, because the team's pride is on the line.
I haven't spoken to anyone who likes the new UI. Do you?
I then have to move my eyes all the way across the page to review any of those other details, through vast fields of whitespace, where I can easily lose my place and end up on a different row.
A whole 160 pixels, 14% of my vertical space, is devoted to a new header which follows you around.
This one is a lot harder to scan quickly or get into your muscle memory.
key_insight: GitHub's notifications redesign sacrifices the efficient, scannable workflow of power users for visual polish and real-time gimmicks, exemplifying how large platforms routinely ship regressions they'll never reverse.
